thalassia.name: Thalassia
thalassia.description: A Shoot'em Up Game made in a team of 4 of which I was the developer. The goal of the project was to create a vertical slice in 6 weeks.
thalassia.pageText:
This is my first long project in a team and the result of a year of learning the Unity engine.

It is my first time at creating a full environment for my level-designer to work with, for placing elements and making the levels easily scalable with somewhat complex interactions and customizable properties.

I designed it profiting of the Unity modules structure and it encouraged me in making my code as modulable as possible going forward.

maze.name: Maze
maze.description: A randomly generated maze game I made using my own maze generation algorithm. This project was my introduction to game development.
maze.pageText:
I created this game for an assignement in highschool that spanned over a few weeks. A friend helped me with the sprites of the background.

It was supposed to be the sequel to a previous game I made, but ended up a completely different concept as I was obsessed by the idea of creating a maze algorithm, idea that would come back years later, this time for optimization purposes.

I had a lot of fun creating the progressively revealing map, as well as the drawable path and waypoint systems.

reactionTime.name: Reaction Time
reactionTime.description: A program whose goal was for me to get used to the C++ language and raw computer graphics with SDL2.
reactionTime.pageText:
It was a very quick project I made over a few days to test my understanding of SDL2.

I made this application because I wanted to see if there was a difference between an online benchmarking website and a very fast C++ program.

rhythmGame.name: Rhythm Game
rhythmGame.description: A project supposed to be a game that ended up being my first attempt at creating a game engine.
rhythmGame.pageText:
This was supposed to be an original game majorly inspired by Muse Dash, but with a playfield where the notes could come from all 4 directions.

The artist I was collaborating with stopped working on the project very early on, so I started instead just making it an SDL2 renderer.

This started my passion for creating a game engine and finding ways to make everything I can as optimized as possible.

first3DRender.name: 3D Render
first3DRender.description: A test project rendering in 3D on the CPU, made to understand vector math. It inspired me to later make a 3D renderer.
first3DRender.pageText:
This is an attempt at rendering 3D geometry by individual pixels on the CPU, using almost entirely custom (innaccurate) math for computing fragment positions.

It's during this project that I understood that I shouldn't try to reinvent the wheel. It made me start using rendering APIs from that point on.

Nonetheless, it served as an introduction in 3D geometry by teaching me the basics about how vectors, matrices and transformations work.

particle2DCollision.name: Particle Collision
particle2DCollision.description: An attempt at creating a simple 2D collision system for particles and walls, later upgraded to a path finder using collisons.

kotono.name: Kotono
kotono.description: A first attempt at making a 3D game engine in C# using OpenGL with OpenTK.
kotono.pageText:
This is the first version of my Kotono Engine, I started this project because I was really starting to get into computer graphics and wanted to really pursue path.

I chose to go with OpenTK in C# as this was the occasion to learn both a new language and the OpenGL graphics system.

It made me dive into all the basics of rendering, shaders, pipelines. I also started to enjoy polymorphism and experimented creating a complex object architecture.

kotonoEngine.name: Kotono Engine
kotonoEngine.description: This is the current iteration of my game engine. Designed with a modular architecture in mind and powered by the Vulkan API.
kotonoEngine.pageText:
This project is based on the object architecture of Kotono, my first iteration of this engine. Which had started to become convoluted and whose structure I reworked every other day.


I chose to remake it entirely from scratch and swapped both the language and rendering system :

 - I chose to replace C# with C++ as I already had some experience using it and wanted to actually learn to and use it well.

 - I also decided to move from OpenGL to the Vulkan API for rendering, as I had already completed the Vulkan Tutorial and was very interested in the philosophy behind it.

twoCrowns.name: Two Crowns (copy)
twoCrowns.description: A reproduction of the game Two Crowns made in a week, the visuals were made by a friend.

mazeGenerator.name: Maze Generator
mazeGenerator.description: A stupidly fast maze generator, I used this project to upgrade my maze generator algorithm I first developed in python.
mazeGenerator.pageText:
The algorithm I used for this project was originally one I created for my randomly generated maze game in python back in high school.

I made this for fun in a few days as a side project during the first year of my game-design bachelor.

I then went back to it a couple of times whenever I learned about new optimizations techniques from C# or other languages I was learning at the time that I could apply to this project.

shipShooter.name: Ship Shooter
shipShooter.description: A little program made to understand how movement works in a realtime game.

osu.name: osu! (copy)
osu.description: A project about recreating the rhythm game "osu!", including saving game data and importing maps from files.
osu.pageText:
The idea for this project was to recreate as much as I could of the rhythm game "osu!" over the span of a month.

I was able to develop most of the main gameplay of playing a beatmap, as well as a system for importing beatmaps from text files and various difficulty modifiers.

It was my very first time working with 2D vectors and making a proper game loop as older games I made all updated states depending on user inputs.

tetris.name: Tetris (copy)
tetris.description: A recreation of the game "Tetris" and my first C++ game, the goal was to understand the basics of SDL2.
tetris.pageText:
This was my first attempt at creating an application that wasn't command line only in C++.

I chose to recreate the game "Tetris" as I thought it was a fairly beginner project for trying out SDL2.

wanderBlossom.name: Wander Blossom
wanderBlossom.description: An original game created in 10 weeks with 3 friends, I was the team's developer. The project that made me love Unreal Engine.
wanderBlossom.pageText:
We were asked for this project to create a retro style action-adventure game using Unreal Engine. We chose to lean into a cartoon-like world.

I helped with the game-design that we drastically changed multiple times to find what we think is the best possible mechanic for the game, the flower umbrella.

During this project, my understanding of the Unreal Engine started to expand a lot. I learned to use concepts like sub-levels to make our open-world idea possible, I learned to optimize active and innactive actors for it.

It also served as a first dive into animation blueprints, cinematics, mesh instancing, relatively complex shaders, particle systems and other concepts.

mcdoMenuGenerator.name: McDo Menu Generator
mcdoMenuGenerator.description: My first application made in Flutter in about 10 days. Get a menu given a calories count target in any McDonald's in France.
mcdoMenuGenerator.pageText:
For this project, I wanted to use the skills I had acquired over the years to complete a project in a short period of time, and that I could actually regularly use for myself.

I chose to use Flutter for it because I wanted to learn something new and it looked like the most adapted choice out there.

It was surprisingly easy to convert the skills I had learned in other programming languages and taught me a lot about interface styling and all the logic behind.

pestacle.name: Pestacle
pestacle.description: A game made with 3 friends over the span of 5 days for the Global Game Jam 2026. This was my first time making a point-and-click game.
pestacle.pageText:
We wanted for this project to create a game that is in an actual releasable state after only the 5 days of development.

This was used as a small break from the production of "Le Souveniste" and a way for our team to gain confidence by finishing a game.


I was the only developer of the team and helped in the game-design, I really wanted to create a mechanic where the player could place actors on a theater stage.

We also made the game easily scalable by adding a shop and splitting the game in levels that correspond to famous theater plays.

karmaPlease.name: Karma, Please
karmaPlease.description: The first game I created during a game jam that took place over a weekend. I was among the developers of the team and made the chat system.
karmaPlease.pageText:
This was my first time working in a team of game developers, it was also the first game I developed using a game engine.

This was a really fun experience that confirmed I wanted to make games.

My role in this project being creating a chat system, I wanted to give my all to the task and found an easy and modulable way for the narrative designer to implement dialogues.

This may not be the greatest project visually, but I am really proud of what I produced with the experience I had.

songe.name: Songe
songe.description: A game made for the Global Game Jam 2025. I was the developer of the team, we had 4 days to make a game with the theme "Bubble".
songe.pageText:
This is the first time I worked using the Unreal Engine inside of a team. I was tasked of all the gameplay programming, it was an intense week that helped me prepare handling future projects.

This was also the first time I finished a game using this engine, and my first real dive into blueprint development and all the Unreal actor system.

As the team was quite bigger than what I had previously worked in, I learned a lot about team communication and project management.

souveniste.name: Le Souveniste
souveniste.description: A game made in a team of 4 people over the span of our third year at e-artsup. We had a total creative liberty.
souveniste.pageText:
This is the game that challenged me in the most ways so far. Over the span of a year I practiced my understanding of the Unreal Engine more than ever before.


This project allowed me to explore a lot of concepts like :

 - Creating, optimizing a static lighting and combining it with dynamically lit elements.

 - Understanding how Unreal's interface system works with Common UI and mutliple input types.

 - Learning a lot of shader concepts for the portal, the apparition feedbacks or the foliage's organic movements.

 - Using the localization system for texts and assets.

vertigo.name: Vertigo
vertigo.description: A VR experience set inside of a single room, where the user will face various vertiginous challenges.
vertigo.pageText:
This spanned over a few weeks and was a collaborative effort among my class of second year of game-design students at e-artsup. We were 12 people on the project and I was one of the 2 developers.

I programmed some of the gameplay mechanics like the player grab in the first experiment, and the rotating room of the third experiment.

It was the first time that I worked on a virtual reality project and it is now being displayed for potential new students on open doors.
